sentences.text: Oh, sure.
My name is Israel Berkenwald, I-S-R-A-E-L B-E-R-K-E-N-B-A-L-D.
No.
April 2, 1923.
In Lodz, Poland.
There were 600,000 the population at large, but the majority of them were Jews. 335,000 Jews.
Yes.
We were five, two brothers and three sisters.
I was the youngest.
Yes.
I can remember my grandparents from my father's side.
Unfortunately, I was too young when my grandparents from my mother's side died.
So I actually don't remember them.
It's like a cloud.
Oh, yeah.
My father's side I remember.
Well, they lived in a small town in Poland called W-A-R-T-A, Warta.
The W in Polish is a hard V, so it's called Warta.
He was a baker.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I used to spend many a summers over there with him.
My grandfather was an orthodox man, so naturally I have to obey by his rules, whereas when I came home, I was used to his rules, but I had to go back to my father's rules, which were not Orthodox.
He was kind of lax as far as religion was concerned.
However, on Saturdays and holidays-- we observed the holidays-- and we had a Kosher home, otherwise our grandparents would never come to visit us.
He did not make tefillin every day, no.
But on holidays, since we used to go a certain synagogue where we met some of our relatives, he made a tefillin, he made a tallit, and he walked in like a very, very observant Jew.
Well, my mother and father, my mother got married when she was 19 years old.
If I remember from stories, she was 19.
And it was a prearranged marriage, of course, like it was in the beginning of the 20th century.
And they were very happy.
We had a fabulous, warm feeling at home.
While we were five children left until 1939, in between there were quite a few more.
How many, I don't know, since I wasn't privy to these informations.
I was the youngest.
My oldest sister was 18 years older than me.
So I wasn't privy to a lot of secrets that the family held.
But what I heard without them telling me or without being privy to listen to and I listened anyway, I know some things.
She was a housewife.
She was very, very loving.
And I think that her life was totally and completely dedicated to her children.
When my sister got married, then two years later my niece was born, I think that I was totally pushed aside.
Because our first-- my mother's first granddaughter was the child to behold, especially since she gave her her mother's name, my grandmother's name.
Which was Hanna, and in Polish, [POLISH].
[POLISH] is sort of an endearing name from Hanna in Polish.
But that doesn't mean that I was pushed aside completely.
What I did in order to solve this problem, I used to go to my sister and sleep over at night.
So I felt like my sister was taking care of me, not that my-- my mother didn't.
He was a shoemaker.
No.
I would say we were a few digits below comfortable, but not poor.
What really happened, what I remember is when I was born, my sisters, two of them, were working already.
They were custom dress makers.
So by the time I was four or five, my youngest sister already was working.
So they sort of helped out.
They did help out.
And when my brother got bigger he helped out, and eventually I started working at the age of 14, when I finished public school.
I graduated public school in Poland.
My father was interested in labor relations.
He was a member of what they called the Bund, a socialist party.
And what they tried to do is sort of fashion themselves after the American labor movement.
However, Poland was not a country where you could be freely a labor leader or be part of a labor movement.
So when it came to a national Polish holiday he sometimes had to hide out, because they were afraid that the labor leaders or so-called labor leaders may speak out loud in public.
So they tried to eliminate him, put him in jail for some reason or another for a couple of days.
I don't remember it, but I was told that my father was twice apprehended and put in jail until the national Polish holiday, which is the 3 of May, which equals the fourth of July here.
That was the holiday that the Polish government usually was very, very much afraid of.
Yes.
One memory was that we had a neighbor, a Polish policeman, and he died.
So in Poland, a funeral became a public scene.
And usually the wealthier Polish people, when they had a funeral, they also hired a band.
So I followed the band when he died.
I followed the band and I was very, very much interested in that band.
Why are they playing music?
And I was walking with them.
Lo and behold, by the time I realized that I'm too far from home, I was in the cemetery.
Little did I know that at home, there was a panic already.
Something had to happen to-- maybe the gypsies [LAUGHS] got me or whatever.
I was five or six years old.
I didn't know how to get home.
And I was terrified of being in a cemetery with a lot of stones all around me.
So I walked out from the cemetery.
I didn't know which way to go home.
And I walked for about three or four hours, crying terribly, till another policeman stopped me.
He says, why are you crying?
Why don't you go home?
I told him, I just don't know how to get home.
And somehow or other, while I was afraid of policemen, this one happened to be a nice man.
He took me by the hand, he took-- went with me to the precinct, and from the precinct, they took a [POLISH].
A [POLISH] is a horse with a carriage, and they took me home crying.
And then--
Yeah, I told them where I lived.
I just didn't tell them that I know how to get home myself.
And then they made my father pay for the [POLISH] and also pay for the [POLISH] for the policeman to go back to his precinct.
That was actually my first encounter with the outside world.
Yes.
It was a Polish school only for Jewish kids.
It was a nice building, just a school.
Because a lot of schools were in private buildings where people used to live there, tenants.
In my case, it was strictly a school that was built by the Jewish agency, but it was strictly for Jewish kids.
A Polish school.
There was no other language but Polish.
They were Jewish, Gentile, and two Germans.
My music teacher was a German, Otto Werner.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then when the war broke out, he wore a band with a swastika, and I had to hide because I wasn't the nicest kid in the class.
So I hid, he shouldn't see me.
Well, music wasn't one of my best subjects.
Oh, yes.
I was very avid reader.
I read almost anything I could get my-- my hands on.
And I did read in the '30s-- I knew already how to read in 1933-- especially I read about '32 or '33 that the [INAUDIBLE] was burning, then that a man called Adolf Hitler is coming to power.
And then the leader of Germany by the name of Hindenburg died, which gave Hitler the power to become the chief of Germany.
And the first thing that I read was that he immediately built a camp, a concentration camp, which was the first one that I heard of, was Dachau.
But somehow or other, the [INAUDIBLE] of science and music simply couldn't build concentration camp where they're taking Jews for no good reason at all simply because they're Jews.
So I sort of figured that either the Polish papers is sort of putting a little bit of [INAUDIBLE] on it, or maybe the Jewish paper is putting a little bit [INAUDIBLE] on it.
I didn't believe that such things are happening until 1938 November, during the Kristallnacht.
Now, everybody is under the impression that Kristallnacht-- crystal night, the night of the broken crystal-- happened in Germany.
Well, 99% probably happened in Germany.
In Lodz where I was born, there was a crystal store by the name of L.
Winters, beautiful store.
And on the main drag of Lodz called [POLISH].
And there was a store called L.
Winters selling crystal and glass and Rosenthal china, and rocks were thrown in to that store.
They're following a Jewish store, yeah, L.
Winters.
Leon Winter was the name of the store.
But that happened the next day, which meant that some of the Polish anti-Semites-- at that time they were called endecki, the club for endecki, E-N-D-E-C-K.
An endeck was an anti-Semite, and he actually followed the teachings of Hitler.
So that was in Lodz.
I'm sure there were other incidents, but that was the only incident that I ran immediately from my house about four blocks away to look what had happened to that store.
And that was the day after crystal night.
It was the middle of the day.
Did that make you think about what might happen later?
I started believing that something is wrong, but I still didn't, because I read already about another camp that is being opened.
They opened-- I think in '35 or '36 they opened Buchenwald.
Then in-- I read another camp was opened strictly for women called Ravensbruck.
And while following these things, I said, my god, something is really wrong over there.
Something is really bad going on.
Well, that's the next.
In 1936, Jews started coming in from Germany.
In fact, right next door to me lives a man that was a friend of mine before the war.
And lo and behold, I met him right here in Golden Lakes village in West Palm Beach.
I looked at him and I said, don't know I know you?
He says, no, how would you know me, I'm a German Jew?
I go, well, I'm a Polish Jew, but you look so familiar.
And we started talking to each other.
I says, well, did you ever come to Poland?
He said, yes, before the war.
I was in Poland for about a year and a half.
I says, and then where did you go, did you go to England?
He says yes, how do you know?
And that's exactly what happened.
They took about 120 kids that were under 16, and they got cards-- [? shift ?] cards or whatever-- to enter England.
And the parents, if they were willing to give up the kids and send them to England, he was one of them.
And he lived only two houses away from me, and I recognized him.
Did your family have any interest in Zionism?
Zionism, no.
Unfortunately, I was the only one that sort of looked to it, but just for my own self.
I didn't want my family to know about it.
I thought to take Israel by force, and I belonged to a club called Betar, which followed the teaching of Vladimir Jabotinsky and Trumpeldor.
So I followed that group, that club, because some of my friends belonged to it.
And I sort of figured it's a nice idea, it's a good idea, finally take it--
I don't know if he would be against it.
I don't think he would look kindly to me belonging to a group that was called the bad or fighting people.
Jews didn't fight.
That was the general idea.
A Jewish kid does not fight, whereas Jabotinsky and Trumpeldor advocated taking back Israel by force.
And in 1946, they became [NON-ENGLISH].
That was the original [NON-ENGLISH], the Betar.
Yes.
We had-- we lived in the heart of the town, and Lodz was a modern city.
So the heart of the town was very modern.
We had running water, we had electricity.
With a string.
But it was not in the house, it was in the hallway.
There were four tenants on the floor, each one shared one toilet.
We had a radio.
That's another heartache that I remember very, very dearly.
In 1939, shortly before I left Lodz, I carried the radio-- it was a [INAUDIBLE], a big box.
I had to carry that into the German headquarters across the street where we lived and give it up because a Jew was not allowed to have a radio.
Oh, do I ever.
Do I ever.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The first day of the war, I was assigned-- since I was already 16-years-old, I was assigned to be a gopher.
In other words, the-- they were preparing for war in '39.
The war wasn't done yet, but the Polish government gave out all kind of orders, what to do and what not to do.
First of all, we had to hide lights from the windows, [INAUDIBLE] from the windows.
That was one of my jobs, to run around the buildings, look if there's light coming out.
There was no war yet.
I used to go with orders-- I mean, I didn't read the orders.
I was getting an envelope to go from this section to this section.
So I was a gopher.
Unfortunately or fortunately, the war came to our town, which is not too far from the German border, within four days.
I saw the first German soldier driving in to the heart of the town where we lived on a motorcycle.
And that was a German.
Well, life-- life ended for us.
It really ended.
Peace and a peaceful life came to a sudden stop, really pressed on the brakes and it stopped.
First of all, we couldn't leave the house after 5 o'clock in the evening.
That was about two or three days later.
But what I did is I-- I had some [INAUDIBLE].
I had a brother-in-law, a doctor, a doctor of medicine.
He studied and got his degree in Nantes, France.
He came back in '36 when he got married.
He married my daughter-- my sister.
In Nantes.
So what happened, there were all kind of rumors.
And the rumors were that when the Germans will come in, the first thing they'll do is anybody over 13 that is a Jew will be killed.
How these rumors started, I don't know.
But the rumors where there, and you were afraid.
So my father decided that all the men in his life, which is two brother-in-laws-- two son-in-laws, my brother, and I should run towards Warsaw.
So for-- that was 110 kilometers, which is approximately 60 to 65 miles, which we did.
How did you travel?
On the way-- by foot.
We walked.
Yeah, we walked.
So we walked toward Warsaw.
My brother got lost, and since he lost us, he just turned around and went back.
That's the only brother I had.
Mendel, [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was his name.
In Jewish it was Mendel.
He had three names, really.
He was sick, so they added a name to him, Chaim, [HEBREW].
[HEBREW] was for long life.
And maybe that's why he recovered.
[LAUGHS]
He went back.
And we didn't know that.
We didn't know.
My brother-in-law, the doctor and I, have reached, the two of us, have reached Warsaw.
And we were in Warsaw for three weeks during the most vicious bombardment that you could ever imagine.
And since he was a doctor, he wore a band stating that he's a doctor.
So they immediately put him into work.
Since he didn't want to lose me, he made me his assistant.
And I helped him in a lot of things, as far as putting band-aids on, roll band-aids, unroll band-aids, see who is still worthwhile saving, who is not.
In most cases, I just covered the body and we walked away, because there was no way we could-- we could take the body.
And once the Germans came in to Warsaw, the fight was over, we walked back to Lodz.
The walking back is a long story.
I-- actually, I remember every day, but we made it.
We made it.
We came home and we found my brother.
We had-- we had assigned to us a cellar.
No, no.
On that long walk back.
On that long-- we did not sleep.
No.
We did not sleep, we did not sleep, we did not sleep.
We walked, and we tried to make it as fast as possible to get into Warsaw to be part of the fight against the Germans.
Walking back was much easier.
Walking back, we were able to hitch sometimes a ride on a horse and wagon.
We did sleep over two or three nights, and we made it back.
Well, when we came back, we found the whole family was in disarray.
In disarray.
My sister, naturally she thought that she lost her husband, and her son was just born while we were away.
So naturally, we went over there.
His name was Julek.
Beautiful little boy, gorgeous little boy.
And I went home, and we found out that so far, nothing has happened to our family.
We are together.
So that was a good thing, and we thought maybe we'll overcome it.
Maybe we will come out unscratched.
How little did I know.
Yes. 5 o'clock, you couldn't walk out into the street, which immediately put a very heavy burden on us.
We couldn't go to the family.
We couldn't see the family.
And if you did walk out into the street, you took a chance of being caught by the Germans for manual labor.
Because they were catching people in the middle of the street, and in some cases, you did work that was unnecessary.
Like they gave you a toothbrush to clean the toilet, an outhouse, to sweep the floor with a toothbrush, or to carry stones.
Just to make us feel Jews.
To put the shame on us.
Yeah, well, it was strictly humiliation, of course.
I got caught three times.
Three times.
I got caught to work, and that's where the story actually begins.
Because my father decided that my brother and I should not stay here any longer, since a lot of people were going towards the Russian border and crossing into the Soviet Union.
My father decided that my brother and I should leave Poland and go into the Russian border and cross into Russia, which we did.
